source.title: Biological and social influences on cognitive control processes dependent on prefrontal cortex

confidence_rationale: Review by Adele Diamond -- the leading authority on EF development and PFC maturation -- providing the biological and social grounding for how play trains EF through PFC engagement. Source quality is high (Progress in Brain Research, expert author). Mapping fidelity is moderate: Diamond reviews EF generally and notes play's importance for EF, but does not specifically dissect games-with-rules vs. other play types. Transfer risk is low.
failure_signatures: Does not distinguish games-with-rules from other play types -- treats play as a unified category for EF purposes.
Review paper without new primary data.
PFC argument is at the level of brain region, not specific circuit mechanism -- cannot map to REE subsystem architecture directly.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Play (along with physical education and the arts) is among the activities that improve EF and frontal-lobe-dependent cognitive control, even without specialists or fancy equipment. EF functions (attentional control, self-regulation, working memory, inhibition) depend on late-maturing PFC and can be improved even in preschoolers.
mapping.ree_translation: Grounds the biological feasibility of MECH-197: play trains EF components precisely because those components depend on PFC, which is still rapidly developing during the games-with-rules stage (ages 4-7). The rule-following demands of games-with-rules place repeated, progressive demands on PFC-dependent inhibitory control (suppress impulse to cheat), working memory (track shared game state, remember whose turn), and cognitive flexibility (adapt to opponent moves). This is the biological substrate for MECH-197's social coordination training claim.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Diamond does not distinguish games-with-rules from other play types or from physical activity. The specific mechanism linking rule-game structure to PFC training is inferred, not demonstrated. The paper provides the neural level but does not specify what game features drive the PFC engagement.
mapping.source_context: Review chapter drawing on neurobiological and developmental psychology literature. Leading expert on EF development.

confidence_components.notes: High-quality review from leading expert. Mapping fidelity reduced because play is treated as a unified category; games-with-rules specificity requires additional inference.
